transcript:
Tushar Jain: Hello everyone. All right. Welcome everyone. We're going to talk today about governance. And as we get into it, I want to talk a little bit about how Solana governance works today. In that, it's pretty ambiguous. It's unclear what things need to be voted on versus not. We've had a few votes in the history of the network, but not everything has been voted on. It's unclear exactly how the votes should be conducted. It's unclear how long do we need to discuss things. There's a lot of opportunities to make things more clear. And this all came to a head earlier this year when we had SIMD-228. SIMD-228 was the biggest governance event in the history of crypto in terms of the amount of voting and engagement. There were tens of billions of dollars of SOL which voted on this inflation reduction proposal. And while as a sponsor of the proposal, the vote didn't go the way that I had hoped, we did learn quite a bit from that experience about the process of governance and what needs to happen in order to improve how Solana governance works in the future. So, today what we're going to introduce to you all is a upgrade to the Solana governance system. And there's two major components of it. One, there is a document that we're proposing called the Solana Constitution, which outlays some of those basic questions like what needs to be voted on, who votes, when do they vote? Simple stuff, but simple is not always easy. And second, there is a new voting app that is going to go live which introduces a concept we call Staker Override, which allows stakers to override their validator's votes. And I think this is a new form of democracy. It's representative democracy with voter override. I think it's going to be extremely powerful. How did we get here? After SIMD-228, as we were absorbing these lessons, we've had a number of very wide consultations with key stakeholders across the industry. We kicked this off at a meeting at Accelerate with a bunch of key validators, core devs, etc. And today we're excited to talk to you about the conclusion of that work, which is the Solana Constitution. Before we get into the details of how all of that works, I want to turn it over to Nick to talk about why should we care?

Nick Almond: Yeah, I'm very pleased that there's still people here because a lot of people don't care about governance. And but I'm going to try for two minutes convince you that you should. Because leaps in governance has created wonders. And I mean that literally. So if you look back through history, we've had various leaps in civilizational capability. A lot of people think this is just like technological advances that have driven this, but actually, it's usually leaps of governance. So the Egyptians, they built the pyramids. You can't just throw labor at this and pyramids happen. There needs to be organization. Governance is how you organize people at scale and it's how you create wonders. Democracy. Athenian democracy is still one of the most interesting things that's ever happened in human civilization. But because of it, it created civic participation. Instead of a pure pyramid of power with pharaohs and emperors at the top, we had civic participation. And that created art and science. It created dialogue. And it's the reason why we still exist in a democracy today, or many of us do. Infrastructure. The Romans, this bridge was built 2,000 years ago. It's a bit of a marvel. And the Romans managed to scale across continent-wide and they did it through governance. They created a very strong military. They created logistics, supply chains. And it was governance and being able to coordinate people at scale that got them to it. These cathedrals, this cathedral was built about 800 years ago. And they used to build things generationally. This church took about, this cathedral took about 300 years to build. And you do that through governance and coordination. And then you can imagine like the Industrial Revolution. This is the point where things really kicked off because this is when we created joint stock companies. This is when we created management sciences. This is how you could not just build something, but manage it for years to come and generate value out of it. And now we're here, right? We've got the internet. This I think is the most radical shift in civilization that we've had since day one. So we've now radically connected every person on the planet communicationally and now blockchains do this with value. But the interesting point is that we don't have the governance system of the internet yet. Tushar said this earlier, it's kind of a feudal system. And I think that's right. But what we can do with blockchains and with things like the Constitution that we're suggesting here is to generate ways in which we can govern each other on the internet, distributed system all over the world. Does anyone know who painted this? It's Banksy. Banksy put this mural on a wall to protest against the censorship, this kind of disturbing censorship regime that's starting to emerge in the UK. And they thought it was a good idea to censor it. So that was a piece of art, now it's a masterpiece. And what happens is, I think we're seeing is the end of an era of governance. Traditional governance is starting to collapse, starting to fail. And we need new ideas. What I've believed for about 10 years now is why I've been so obsessed on crypto and governance is that I truly believe that the next great leap in governance is going to come from the world of decentralized systems. It's going to come from blockchains. It's going to come from interesting experiments where we try radical new forms of democracy. And I think actually, as Tushar said, this is an interesting new form of democracy with delegation with voter override where you can revoke your votes away from your delegates. I think there's quite a lot of people in the world that would like to be able to do that at the moment. And over to Tushar again.

Tushar Jain: So, I know that we all understand how important it is to build things and that's been the core of Solana's culture. But it's also important to manage the things that we have built in the way that creates the most value for the most number of people. And that's what we think governance can help accomplish. As Nick described some of those examples, governance is a critical component of utilizing what we have built to create the most value for the most people. So let me tell you a little bit about what practically are we proposing with the new Solana Constitution. The first thing that we're proposing is what we call Stake Sovereignty. This means that ultimately stakers control the network. Stakers get to decide who they are staking to, but in addition to deciding who they're staking to, there's this new concept called Staker Override. So what this means is on a given proposal that's going to be voted on, a staker can vote even if their validator did not vote. Or if the validator voted yes and the staker wants to say no, they can override their validator's vote with their percentage of the stake. We think this is a really powerful innovation that dramatically solves the principal-agent problem that can exist in a validator representative democracy type system. The validators are absolutely critical components of governance. They are tasked with actually running the network code honestly. They are also tasked with what we're calling stewardship. And that means bringing to the network's attention when a controversial change is proposed. And I'll describe what that practically means in just a second when we introduce this new concept. You are all probably familiar with SIMDs. SIMDs have been the Solana Improvement Documents that have existed since the beginning of the network. And they've governed how updates to Solana have worked in the past. But they were subject to a bunch of that confusion I was mentioning earlier. Which SIMDs need to be voted on? Which ones don't? Who votes? When do you vote? All this stuff. So what we are proposing with the Constitution is a new type of proposal called a Solana Governance Proposal, or an SGP. An SGP is a detailed proposal for a systemic change to the network. SGPs are voted on. SIMDs are not voted on. How do we decide if something is systemic? We can't define it. It's really hard to do. We tried. The way we define that something is systemic is if enough of you as stakers and community members believe it is systemic. And the way we do that is we allow stakers and validators to escalate any proposal to label it systemic. And only proposals that are labeled systemic can be voted on in order to prevent voter fatigue and overvoting. Think of the SGPs as a specification of what to build and a general direction for the network. Then we have SIMDs, which continue to exist as they have. They're a technical specification for a proposed network update. These sail through opportunistically. So if a SIMD is proposed, it just goes through the current process. There's no voting on SIMDs. Core devs propose it, it goes through technical peer review, and it sails on through. SGPs are voted on. We don't want to slow core devs down. But if a SIMD is contentious and it says that there's going to be some change that the network thinks is systemic, then 15% of stake can label that change systemic and it escalates the SIMD into an SGP and forces a vote on that issue. So in order to lay this out a little more visually and clearly, I'll hand it over to Nick to take you through this flowchart.

Nick Almond: Thanks Tushar. Bit late in the day for flowcharts, I know, but I'll give it a go. So it's a four-phase process and you can think of the first being how do we make the proposals? So we're going to do this all on GitHub. We have a forum. We want to collect this all into a very sort of robust place that we can discuss proposals. We have these two proposal tracks. They go through the usual technical review. And the important bit here is that decision-making process: is this a systemic proposal? In which case it goes into the SGPs. So this would be things like MCP. We have AlpenGlow for example. Big systemic changes to the network that materially change the economics, they materially are consensus breaking changes, we vote on them. So this is the kind of elevation process. It's completely permissionless. The validators elevate a proposal if 15% of stake decides something is worthy of voting on, then it's voted on. And then once, as soon as that proposal threshold is hit, it triggers an on-chain mechanism. So previously we kind of did this with proposal specific tokens. It was a bit hacky. Now we've, this is the kind of major mechanistic upgrade of the protocol. We have a new voting program that uses some interesting technology, which I'll talk about in a second, but as soon as that hits, it triggers this process. So the discussion period is on-chain. We deliberated in consultation processes how many epochs this should be, but 10 epochs of discussion. And as soon as that concludes, a snapshot is taken. So this actually uses a cool bit of tech which Jito created called an NCN, which is a decentralized snapshotting process. Looks at a specific slot, calculates everyone's stake, we reach consensus on it, and then it triggers the voting window, which at the moment is down for three epochs. And this is going to be still live so we can debate this a little bit further. But effectively we have this epoch by epoch tallying process where stakers can override their validators and validators vote. And then once it's finished, we finalize the proposal. And this is the kind of critical piece. We want all of these proposals to sail through. We don't want to overvote. Primarily this is a veto mechanism. So the strongest signal that you can get on the network is that we're over quorum of 33%, 33% of stake has turned up, and we have a supermajority on that. And that's the highest degree of signal that you can get on the network and there's a very strong mandate for it. If you're under quorum and you get quorum failure, we treat that as indifference. It just means the network didn't necessarily care that much. So that's the process. And I think it puts us in a really good position.

Tushar Jain: It puts us in a position for Solana to be the largest decentralized governance system in the world. And like Nick mentioned, governance innovation is happening here in the blockchain industry. The whole world needs more governance innovation. We're using Enlightenment Age, you know, 1700s based governance systems across the world today. And we no longer live in that era. We live in the Information Age and we should have an Information Age native governance system. But we have to run the experiments somewhere and this is the place to do it. So let me just end with what happens next? What do we do? Well, first, this voting app is going to be released in Q1. And the first vote is going to be the ratification... I think they've killed us. Oh wow. I'm still going. Anyway, so the SGP-1 will be the ratification of the Constitution. We are still taking input on some of those variables, the structure, and ideas for the community to debate. Once the Constitution is ratified, we have some ideas for SGP-2. Those of you who are on Twitter might have seen Mert hint some governance changes that he wants to do. So we think that might be SGP-2. So if you're excited about this and you have some feedback about the Constitution, please feel free to, you know, I think the best place to have this conversation is on Twitter. Tag us, put up a bunch of comments, and let's continue the discussion there. Thank you everybody.

Nick Almond: Thanks everyone.

sources.title: How BAM extends Solana’s Lead: Making Internet Capital Markets A Reality | BAM
sources.snippet:
[Back to Blog](https://bam.dev/blog/)

November 12, 2025 • 8 minute read

# How BAM extends Solana’s Lead: Making Internet Capital Markets A Reality

Lucas Bruder

Jito Labs

Share

- _Solana's long-term success depends on deterministic execution infrastructure that scales transparently as the network grows. BAM exists to ensure that as Solana scales, the execution layer remains transparent, fair, and aligned with the applications and users who drive network growth._
- _BAM provides cryptographically verifiable transparency for sequencing that no other solution can provide: every ordering decision proven, not promised._
- _BAM will help everyone using or building on Solana succeed:_
 - _Applications gain the execution certainty needed to offer tighter spreads and predictable fills through Application Controlled Execution (ACE)._
 - _Traders benefit from superior execution quality that rivals or exceeds other venues._
 - _Validators get open-source infrastructure they can verify. When applications succeed on Solana, validators succeed._

# What Is BAM?

Solana stands alone: the only blockchain where trading SOL is cheaper and faster on-chain than on centralized exchanges. With BAM, that lead widens as apps gain the tools to outprice and out-execute other exchanges. BAM provides the execution infrastructure necessary to make on-chain trading not just competitive with centralized venues, but categorically superior.

The Block Assembly Marketplace (BAM), which Jito Labs revealed in July, represents the evolution toward a more transparent and application-driven transaction processing paradigm. BAM addresses a fundamental challenge that has constrained the network's growth: the inability for applications to provide predictable, high-quality execution guarantees to their users.

# Why Solana's Block Building Needed to Evolve

We’ve detected at least seven different transaction schedulers operating on Solana, each with distinct ordering logic and timing characteristics. This fragmentation creates fundamental uncertainty for applications and traders who depend on predictable execution. Traders cannot determine whether they're receiving fair execution or being systematically disadvantaged by opaque scheduling decisions made by validators with competing interests. The result is wider spreads and reduced liquidity as market makers price in execution uncertainty, while developers face inconsistent environments that undermine their ability to compete with centralized alternatives.

# Why Jito Labs Is Building BAM

Jito Labs helped pioneer trading infrastructure on Solana through our Block Engine. While these systems created significant value for validators and improved transaction execution, we've also watched how they've evolved. We've seen concentration dynamics emerge, observed the pressure toward private orderflow relationships, and recognized that without intervention, Solana risks following the same path toward opacity that degraded Ethereum's execution layer.

Ethereum's experience with proposer-builder separation (PBS) offers particularly instructive lessons. While PBS introduced competitive block building, it created perverse incentives: builders maximize MEV extraction to win block auctions, often at users' expense. This dynamic leaves the network stuck in a positive feedback loop that can be hard to unwind. Increased sandwich attacks and other forms of extractive MEV pushed orderflow private, as users sought protection through direct builder relationships. Builders offered rebates for exclusive access to this orderflow, and those with the most private flow could pay higher auction fees, winning more blocks and attracting even more exclusive deals. The result is a concentration of power among a small number of builders, increased opacity in transaction processing, and timing games that degraded network performance. Most concerningly, some dominant builders were operated by trading firms with direct financial incentives to exploit

tags.impact_reason: A proposal for a Solana Constitution and a new voting app with staker override functionality represents a major upgrade to the governance system.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a monumental, glowing constitutional tablet with an integrated 'veto' switch as the Semantic Anchor. This object directly visualizes the story's two core elements: the creation of a formal 'Solana Constitution' and the introduction of a tangible 'Staker Override' mechanism. The tablet's classical form conveys the gravity of governance, while its crystalline, high-tech material grounds it in the world of blockchain. I included the diegetic text 'VETO' as a holographic label to emphasize this new, direct power in a way that feels native to the futuristic aesthetic.
visual_idea.description: In a vast, dark chamber, a monumental tablet made of semi-translucent, glowing crystal stands vertically, representing the Solana Constitution. Its surface is etched with faint, luminous circuits. Embedded into the center of the tablet is a heavy, brushed titanium mechanical lever switch—the 'Staker Override'. A crimson-red holographic label reading 'VETO' glows next to the switch, casting a sharp light. The scene is lit with dramatic, high-contrast lighting, catching the sharp edges of the crystal and the metallic grain of the lever. The background fades into deep, out-of-focus server racks, suggesting the immense scale of the network.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A monumental, vertical tablet, crafted from semi-translucent, glowing crystal, stands in a vast, dark chamber, representing the Solana Constitution. Its surface is intricately etched with faint, luminous circuits that subtly pulse. Embedded directly into the crystalline center is a heavy, robust mechanical lever switch, meticulously fashioned from brushed titanium—this is the 'Staker Override'. Adjacent to the switch, a crimson-red holographic label reading 'VETO' emits a sharp, intense glow, casting crisp highlights. The entire scene is bathed in dramatic, high-contrast Cinematic Chiaroscuro lighting, masterfully catching the razor-sharp edges of the glowing crystal and dramatically accentuating the metallic grain and texture of the brushed titanium lever. The background recedes into deep, out-of-focus server racks, emphasizing the immense, epic scale of the underlying network, with ample negative space at the top to convey monumental height. Vertical aspect ratio 3:4, hyperrealistic, photoreal detail, 8K ultra-detailed, realistic materials, physically based rendering (PBR), ray-traced optics, octane render, hyper-detailed.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a monumental, glowing constitutional tablet with an integrated 'veto' switch as the Semantic Anchor. This object directly visualizes the story's two core elements: the creation of a formal 'Solana Constitution' and the introduction of a tangible 'Staker Override' mechanism. The tablet's classical form conveys the gravity of governance, while its crystalline, high-tech material grounds it in the world of blockchain. I included the diegetic text 'VETO' as a holographic label to emphasize this new, direct power in a way that feels native to the futuristic aesthetic.
visual_idea.description: An extreme macro shot focusing on the mechanical 'Staker Override' switch. The camera angle is tight on the brushed titanium lever, showing its precise engineering and texture as it's embedded within the glowing, micro-etched crystalline surface of the constitutional tablet. A powerful key light glints off the metal's edge, while the internal light of the tablet creates a dramatic underglow, highlighting the point of contact between the two materials. The focus is on the tangible mechanism of power, poised for activation.

final_prompt.prompt_text: An extreme macro shot, tightly framed and perfectly symmetrical in a 1:1 square aspect ratio, focusing on a mechanical 'Staker Override' switch. The composition centers on a precisely engineered brushed titanium lever, with the words 'Staker Override' intricately micro-etched onto its metallic surface, showcasing its texture in hyper-detail. This robust lever is deeply embedded within the glowing, micro-etched crystalline surface of a monumental constitutional tablet, filling the entire frame with rich textures and intricate details. A powerful cinematic chiaroscuro key light dramatically glints off the metal's edge, creating sharp highlights and deep, pronounced shadows characteristic of high-contrast lighting. Concurrently, the internal light of the crystalline tablet emits a dramatic underglow, vividly illuminating the precise point of contact between the two materials and emphasizing the switch's tangible mechanism of power, poised for activation. Every surface is rendered with photorealistic precision and physically based rendering, evoking the aesthetic of a digital monument. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, cinematic lighting, dramatic contrast, PBR.

editorial.headline: Solana Unveils Constitution and 'Staker Override' to Formalize Network Governance
editorial.standfirst: A new dual-track proposal seeks to resolve the 'principal-agent' problem by granting stakers direct veto power over validators. The move transitions Solana from ambiguous oversight to a structured, constitutionally defined democracy.
key_takeaways: New 'Staker Override' mechanism allows token holders to reverse their validator's vote on contentious proposals.
Governance splits into two tracks: Technical updates (SIMDs) and Systemic changes (SGPs), with a 15% stake threshold to escalate technical items to a vote.
The new voting application launches in Q1, with the ratification of the Constitution set as the first Solana Governance Proposal (SGP-1).
editorial.article_body_markdown:
At Breakpoint 2025, Multicoin Capital’s **Tushar Jain** and Jito’s **Nick Almond** introduced the **Solana Constitution**, a pivotal framework designed to mature the network’s decision-making from early-stage ambiguity to institutional-grade clarity. Following the contentious SIMD-228 inflation reduction vote earlier this year—which saw tens of billions of dollars in SOL participate—the ecosystem recognized the urgent need for defined rules of engagement. The proposed Constitution replaces the current 'feudal' dynamic with a transparent system that delineates exactly what requires a network-wide vote and what remains a technical implementation.

The most significant source of 'Alpha' for institutional participants is the introduction of **Staker Override**. This mechanism directly addresses the principal-agent problem inherent in delegated proof-of-stake systems. Historically, validators held the sole voting power, acting as proxies for their delegators. Under the new system, stakers retain ultimate sovereignty: if a validator votes against their delegators' interests—or fails to vote at all—the capital owners can override that decision with their specific stake weight. This creates a new form of 'representative democracy with voter override,' ensuring that the economic majority retains the final check on protocol direction.

Structurally, the proposal introduces a dual-track governance model to prevent voter fatigue while ensuring security. Routine technical upgrades will continue as **Solana Improvement Documents (SIMDs)**, passing through peer review without a broad vote. However, 'Systemic' changes—those affecting economics or consensus—will be classified as **Solana Governance Proposals (SGPs)** and mandate a network-wide vote. Crucially, the system includes a permissionless safety valve: if 15% of the network stake flags a technical SIMD as 'systemic,' it is automatically escalated to an SGP, forcing a formal vote. This ensures that controversial changes cannot slip through as minor technical updates.

This shift represents a critical evolution for Solana as it positions itself as the execution layer for global finance. For institutional investors, the Constitution offers something vital: predictability. By formalizing the governance process and launching a dedicated voting application in Q1—powered by Jito’s decentralized snapshot technology—Solana is moving toward an 'Information Age' governance model capable of coordinating internet-scale capital markets.
